Fine-grained sketch-based image retrieval (FG-SBIR) emphasizes fully utilizing the faithful nature of sketches to conduct instance-level retrieval.
Sketch has long been established as a worthy query modality complementary to text, conceived as a category-level retrieval task.
Photos are used to enrich the latent space of FG-SBIR models by distilling instance-wise discriminative knowledge of a teacher pre-trained on unlabelled photos.
Existing FG-SBIR models work around a cross-modal triplet objective to learn a discriminative embedding to conduct retrieval.
The general intuition is to make a sketch sit closer to its paired photo while pushing away non-matching ones.
Sketch is paired with photo, and a sketch should sit closer to its paired photo while pushing away non-matching ones.

Conventional triplet setup does not enforce sufficient separation amongst different photos or sketch instances, largely due to the failure of the conventional objective to retain the holistic latent space geometry.
Utilizing an intra-modal triplet objective along with the regular cross-modal triplet objective is important for learning within-triplet feature separation.
The best morphological operations are those that operate on visual attributes bearing relevance with information exclusive to sketches, such as shape.
One of the pressing problems is the scarcity of sketch data, which hinders extra performance gain.
Instead of going for the more explicit route of photo to pseudo sketch synthesis, leveraging unlabelled photos is recommended.
The knowledge distillation setup involves training a model on unlabelled photos only via an intra-modal triplet loss.
The knowledge distillation setup distills instance-wise discriminative knowledge to a FG-SBIR model.
In a cross-modal problem like ours, it is essential to preserve the instance-wise separation amongst photos and sketches.
Contextual similarity needs to be preserved between instances and their nearest neighbors amongst photos and sketches.
The student model distills contextual similarity from the pre-trained teacher photo model in the knowledge distillation setup.

Two frontiers of research have emerged - self-supervised and semi-supervised learning.
Common approaches in semi-supervised learning include entropy minimization and pseudo-labeling.
Pseudo-labeling and consistency regularization are techniques used to improve model performance in semi-supervised learning.

Consistency regularization and pseudo-labeling are both techniques used in machine learning to handle unlabelled data. Pseudo-labeling creates artificial labels for unlabelled data based on confident predictions from trained classifiers, while consistency regularization promotes consistency in predictions between different views of unlabelled data.

Consistency regularization and knowledge distillation are related paradigms in machine learning. Knowledge distillation involves transferring knowledge from a pre-trained teacher network to a student, while consistency regularization promotes consistency in predictions between different views of unlabelled data.

Knowledge distillation and self-distillation are both techniques used in machine learning to transfer knowledge from a teacher model to a student model. Self-distillation employs the same network for both student and teacher models, while knowledge distillation aims at transferring knowledge from a pre-trained teacher network to a student.

Knowledge distillation and multi-exit strategy are techniques used in machine learning for adaptive inference. Knowledge distillation transfers knowledge from a pre-trained teacher network to a student, while multi-exit strategy optimizes compute via multiple exits at different depths for adaptive inference.

PVT involves reshaping for down-scaling individual patch tokens

PVT involves reshaping a fixed number of individual patch tokens to the subsequent feature map, which would be imbalanced on adding one extra token. Therefore, we focus on the second step of PVT-block involving transformer layer involving transformer layer, which can accommodate a variable number of tokens.
Accordingly, at every level, ∆ is fed to the transformer layer concatenated with rest of the N image patch i=1 ∈ RN×d), to obtain the resultant set of tokens.

The student outputs two features: a discriminative (f ∈ Rd), and a distillation feature (µ ∈ Rd). The student's discriminative feature is used to train over a combination of cross-modal and intra-modal triplet losses on labelled data.

Experiments explore other photo augmentation techniques for LIMp
The photo augmentation techniques, including colour distortion, partial blurring and random shift in sharpness, are explored for LIMp (Eqn. 2) in the context of outperforming certain scores.
Morphological augmentations are confirmed to better direct the loss objective by instilling discrimination based on sketch-exclusive information.
The importance of the Distillation Token is justified through the exploration of two designs, showing the need for a dedicated distillation token for knowledge distillation.

Engaging the distillation token at every level via residual connection instills the inductive bias modelled by the pyramidal downscaling into the distillation token via transformer layers, creating a better representation.

The paper introduces a strong baseline for FG-SBIR with PVT backbone and focuses on learning from unlabelled data in FG-SBIR by distilling knowledge from unlabelled photos.
The proposed intra-modal triplet loss increases feature separation in the model's latent space.
An EMA paradigm stabilizes the training of the model.
The paper introduces a distillation token in PVT architecture explicitly catering to knowledge distillation.
Extensive experiments show that the method outperforms existing frameworks and various baselines, proving its significance.
